adaptation_notes: Extracted from full blueprint (legacy nickandme.json). story_beats derived 1:1 from veo_clips; refine dramatic_function/summary by hand. source_excerpts matched from book_text_pages_*.txt by dialogue/visual keyword overlap.

global_production_variables.directorial_treatment: Shot on 35mm anamorphic lenses, high-contrast cinematic lighting, rich shadow detail, film grain, steady tracking camera movements

Character_P.description: Adult man, 28, thin wiry frame, medium height, short dark brown hair, thoughtful brown eyes, slightly tired face, usually a red pizza-delivery polo or light blue t-shirt and jeans. Wardrobe has no name tag, no embroidered name, no ID badge; plain unmarked shirts only.
Character_P.name_reveal_note: Given name withheld until late in the book; never show on-screen name tags/badges before the reveal beat. Use Character_P token only in early visual prompts.
Character_P.voice_profile: American male, late 20s, Midwestern working-class, thin wiry speaker; soft mid baritone, slightly breathy and tired, thoughtful measured pace about 130-145 words per minute, gentle not booming, warm but weary, slight dry irony; consistent every scene.

Character_N.description: Older brother, 31, tall muscular build, reddish-brown hair, strong jaw, intense eyes, intimidating but protective presence; often gym clothes, factory workwear, or casual street clothes.
Character_N.voice_profile: American male, early 30s, tall muscular presence; deeper lower baritone, denser chest voice, confident sometimes aggressive edge, faster punchy cadence about 140-160 wpm when angry, slower when protective; rougher Midwestern working-class timbre than his younger brother; same voice every scene — never switch to a thin or boyish voice.

Character_M.description: Elderly mother, mid-60s to 70s, thin frail frame, brown hair going gray, soft lined face, often house clothes or a simple cardigan; increasingly confused distant eyes; moves carefully, sometimes reaches for walls for balance.
Character_M.voice_profile: American female, mid-60s to 70s, frail elderly mother; thin soft higher voice with age, slower slightly unsteady cadence, Midwestern, gentle or confused not theatrical; consistent elderly female voice every scene.

Character_S.description: Young woman near 28, calm intelligent presence, light blue ocean eyes, pale skin, short brown hair, soft features, often casual tops (greens/neutrals) or simple dresses; empathetic expression, steady posture.
Character_S.voice_profile: American female, late 20s, calm intelligent; warm clear mezzo, soft confident diction, unhurried thoughtful pace about 130-150 wpm, light Midwestern, kind not breathy-childish; same voice every scene.

Kevin McCleary.description: Adult man, mid-40s, solid build, short neat brown hair, practical work clothes, calm supervisory presence, weathered but kind face.
Kevin McCleary.voice_profile: American male, mid-40s supervisor; steady practical mid baritone, calm professional, moderate pace, Midwestern workaday; consistent adult male supervisory voice.

Bob.description: Adult man, late 40s to early 50s, average build, thinning hair, friendly service-worker look, simple polo or work shirt, approachable everyday face.
Bob.voice_profile: American male, late 40s to early 50s; friendly average mid baritone, casual approachable, easy pace, everyday Midwestern; consistent every scene.

Character_P_Young.description: Boy about 8-9 years old, child proportions (NOT adult), small thin frame, short dark brown hair, curious open face, often a simple red or blue t-shirt and jeans; same family features as adult Character_P but clearly a child. No name tag or embroidered name on clothing.
Character_P_Young.name_reveal_note: Given name withheld until late in the book; never show on-screen name tags/badges before the reveal beat. Use Character_P token only in early visual prompts.
Character_P_Young.voice_profile: American boy about 8-9, thin small child; higher light boy voice not adult, curious soft, faster light cadence; NEVER adult male voice; consistent child voice in flashbacks.

Character_N_Young.description: Boy about 12 years old, sturdy preteen build (NOT adult bodybuilder), short reddish-brown hair, confident posture, often dirty white/gray t-shirt and jeans; same family features as adult Character_N but clearly a child/preteen.
Character_N_Young.voice_profile: American boy about 12, sturdy preteen; higher than adult Nick but lower than little brother, confident kid energy, not adult baritone, not tiny toddler; consistent preteen male voice.

Character_P_Teen.description: Teen boy about 15 years old, still thin wiry frame, short dark brown hair, youthful face (NOT full adult), often gym shorts or casual t-shirt; recognizable as younger Character_P. No name tag or embroidered name on clothing.
Character_P_Teen.name_reveal_note: Given name withheld until late in the book; never show on-screen name tags/badges before the reveal beat. Use Character_P token only in early visual prompts.
Character_P_Teen.voice_profile: American male mid-late teens, thin; young voice cracking toward adult, softer than Nick, not full adult baritone yet; consistent teen male voice.

Character_N_Teen.description: Teen / young man about 16-18 years old, already muscular but not 31-year-old adult bulk, reddish-brown hair, often black leather jacket or casual tough clothes; recognizable as younger Character_N, not a middle-aged man.
Character_N_Teen.voice_profile: American male mid-late teens, athletic; deeper teen baritone building toward adult Nick, confident; not little-kid voice; consistent teen male voice.

scenes.setting: Day 1 - Narrator's Apartment / Moxie's Gym
scenes.summary: Day 1 - Narrator's Apartment / Moxie's Gym — Big brother was mine. Not that stupid book, but my real Big Brother. Nick was the guy everybody was scared of.

source_excerpts.excerpt:
Total Pages: 94
--- PAGE 1 ---
 Nick and Me

1 
Big brother was mine. I’m not talking about that stupid book we had to read in junior 
high about how the world was gonna turn weird and everybody could see everything you were 
doing all the time. No, I’m talking about my real Big Brother. Nick was the guy everybody was 
scared of. He had a pair on him the size of steel wrecking balls. His face looked like a puzzle 
with the pieces held together with sticky stuff from a glue gun. He wasn’t ugly, just had a few 
brawls over the years to give him that rough look. It was like he rolled out of bed and forgot to 
comb his hair. A few scars here and there, chin and over his left eyebrow. When he walked in the 
bar, or the gym, people took notice, and man, did they move out of the way. I lived with him and 
Ma after dad took off when I was about eight. We lived downtown in a big brick apartment 
building on Kirk Street. A fifth-floor walk-up, so it was a real pain in the ass to haul up the 
groceries and stuff. Luckily, when I went to the store with Nick, he could load up with about six 
of those shopping bags and take them up in one trip, like he was in one of those “world’s 
strongest man” competitions, where they throw boulders over walls, or pull cars with just their 
teeth. I held the rest. 
 One time, when we went into Moxie’s, the gym down on the corner, one of those guys in 
there was doin’ preacher curls with a 40-pound bar on the curl bench. He was right in the middle 
of a set, but as soon 
--- PAGE 2 ---
weight lifting, but I aint gonna curl no fifty pounds as a warm-up. Hell, I aint gonna curl a 50-
pound weight probably ever. That’s okay though, Nick is my brother, like I said. I know he’ll 
stand up for me no matter what. 
 Milwaukee is where we live. It’s where we lived all of our lives. Right there, on Kirk 
Street in that fifth floor apartment. We used to play kick ball in the alley behind the building and 
set up bases with dented-in garbage can lids. One time little Mark Conner kicked a ball right 
through old Mrs. Engel’s kitchen. Man, was she pissed. Mark was scared to death, but Nick 
stepped right up to her and said he was the one who did it. She called my Ma, and Ma had to pay 
for the window. Somehow Nick gave her cash to pay for it about a week later. I have no idea 
where a 12-year old came up with sixty bucks, but I was sure proud of him for that. He never 
told Ma that it was really Mark who did it. None of us did. 
 The thing about Nick was, he was a real-life Viking - you know? What I mean is we had 
these ancestors that came from Norway or Sweden or somewhere over there in Viking land and 
they came over here and stayed in Wisconsin. So, we are Vikings too, I guess. You never hear 
about Vikings, but they are the biggest bad-asses on the planet. I don’t care about gangsters from 
Mexico or China, or even Irish people. Hell, all they do is drink anyways. What I’m talking 
about are tall, strong people who survived in the middle of winter all the time. 
--- PAGE 3 ---
the way those Vikings used to live over there it makes me all proud. They had those huge swords 
and when somebody died, they floated them out to sea on a raft and set them on fire. That’s the 
way it should be. None of this taking up space with caskets and shit. Just set me on fire and float 
me out on a raft. It is so cool to see fire on top of water. 
 I guess that’s why people respect Nick and our family, even though dad took off.

--- PAGE 46 ---
 “No.” she said calmly. “Why do you let other people bother you like that? You know 
they’re just kids, and there’s nothing funny about what we’re doing, so, just ignore them.” 
 “Well I know that logically, but it still pisses me off” I didn’t know how to describe my 
symptoms of inner rage. 
 “Well, I’ll tell you something. Only when we become masters of ourselves, can we really 
deal with the world. What I mean is, the world is full of stupid, violent, seemingly useless 
people. But you aren’t stupid, violent, or useless. You know more than they do. You can see that 
your feelings are not logical, and you can deal with moving on. You have a purpose. We have to 
live with all kinds of people that aren’t as advanced as we are spiritually.” 
 “Do you really think we are more advanced spiritually? How do you know they aren’t 
yet, but might become like Buddha or something?” 
 “So if they do become like Buddha, we should definitely leave them alone, so they can 
follow that path. I mean, if you think one of those boys is going to become the next spiritual 
master, then you should be glad you got a glimpse of what they used to be like!” She just started 
laughing, and so did I. Thinking about one of those numbskulls becoming enlightened made me 
laugh. 
 “You’re really something, you know that?” 
 “You’re really something too.” She looked up at me with her ocean eyes and I leaned 
down and kissed her. That kiss was like slow motion. It was like there was no one else around, 
and
--- PAGE 47 ---
 “That is one of our missions, I think.” She looked pensive. “We have to learn how to 
speak it to everyone, even strangers, and even people we don’t particularly like.” 
 “That’s going to be hard” I said. 
 “I know. That is the meaning of “loving kindness”. That is the ability to open our hearts 
and accept everyone, even those kids on the corner laughing at us. It might take my whole life, 
but I am bound and determined to find that inside of me and bring it out.” 
 “Let me know if you do, because if my girlfriend is loving everyone, I think I’m going to 
need a new one!” I started laughing. 
 “I could never love anyone like I love you.” That was it. That was what had not been 
said, and now it was said. I would remember the way she looked at me for the rest of my life. My 
girlfriend told me she loved me, and it was the most sincere statement I have ever heard. It really 
came from her heart. 
 “I could never love anyone the way I love you either.” We just smiled at each other and 
kept on walking.

--- PAGE 48 ---
 4

Nick was laughing so hard, I thought he might fall off his couch. He had smoke 
sputtering out of the corner of his mouth, and his face was beet red as usual. At that moment I 
wondered why I had even taken the time to come over to his apartment. 
 “Come on, Bro. You haven’t been to my place for ages.” His argument finally persuaded 
me to ride over to his place and sit around with him and a couple old friends before heading to 
Joe’s to play pool and hassle the waitresses. This was the usual scene: Nick invites people over, 
Nick gets hammered, and Nick has an audience to tell his stories to. Boring. I’ve heard all this 
shit about a million times. 
 “Man,” Bob said holding the ceramic pipe and blowing smoke, “I don’t know about you 
guys, but I’m needin’ some new action.

--- PAGE 41 ---
 “So, what’s up with you?” he said, pressing his lips into a strange pursed curl. 
 “Ah, not much. Just working and thinking about going to school” I said. I was expecting 
a huge knee-slapping laugh from Nick, but he just stared at me silently contemplating what I had 
just said. 
 “Going to school? How the hell are you going to pay for that? And what for?” His look 
displayed an incomprehensible expression, as if he had just heard Ma was pregnant or 
something. 
 “Well, I decided that there’s a lot of good jobs out there, but I have to have a degree to do 
anything worth doing, so I’m going to the community college and getting a bachelor’s degree in 
business. Maybe I can manage a restaurant or someplace like that.” I waited for his arguments. 
 “How the hell will you pay for it? How the hell are you going to have time to watch Ma if 
your screwing around with those pencil-pushers?” His eyes looked like they were set on fire. 
 “Sionna told me I could qualify for a low-interest student loan. I checked it all out, and 
applied for it, and they gave me the money. I mean, really, community college isn’t that much 
compared to other places.” 
 Nick cut me short. “Whoa, whoa, whoa dude. You have really done it now. I can’t 
believe you are letting this chick tell you what to do. It’s not like she’s gonna be around to help 
pay this shit back! That’s all on your head man. I can tell you one thing, it sure ain’t gonna be me 
helping your sorry ass out when you finish – IF you fini
--- PAGE 42 ---
want to work in a pizza place my whole life. Second, I don’t do everything Sionna tells me, this 
was my idea. And lastly, it would really mean a lot to me if you could just step up and be on my 
side for once, you know? You are my brother, man.” I was trying not to lose my cool composure, 
because we really needed to work things out. I needed him to work with me on this. 
 “Be on your side? Be on your side?” he said with an elevating voice. 
 “Man, do you actually remember your own life? How many times have I saved your ass? 
I mean really, can you keep count?” 
 Ma was starting to notice there was something unusual going on. She looked up with a 
confused, creased forehead. She started nodding her head side to side, as if to say, “Please don’t 
argue boys.” Slowly, she returned her eyes back to the television screen and folded her hands in 
her lap. 
 “I know, Nick, I know. I’m not saying that. What I mean is, I really need my family to 
support me on this. It’s going to be really hard, and take a lot of time. I will be here for Ma. 
Classes aren’t all day. I have to be in and out of here. I’ll make sure she’s okay.” 
 Nick looked pensive. “Do you remember that time in high school when Donny Amstel 
started harassing you? He was pushing you in the locker room after gym class and calling here 
hanging up, and threatening to steal you bike? Do you remember all that?” 
 “Yeah, sure, I do remember that. You told me you would take care of it, and then, all of a 
sudden, he just 
--- PAGE 43 ---
my body in his front door, so he couldn’t close it. Oh God man, he looked like he was about to 
shit his pants.” Nick started giggling like a little kid. “Then – then, oh man. Then, I told him if he 
ever threatened you like that again he would be killed. I told him our family had mafia 
connections. And he believed me! He was so scared, I couldn’t believe he actually believed me. 
What a dumbass!

Character_N_Young and Character_P_Young flinch, worried. Clear kick, flight, window impact.

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: One time little Mark Conner kicked a ball right through old Mrs. Engel's kitchen. Man, was she pissed. Mark was scared to death.

story_beats.visual_event: FLASHBACK alley OTS: camera behind Character_N_Young (12, reddish-brown hair, dirty t-shirt, jeans, child NOT adult). He faces brick house; Mrs. Engel yells from shattered kitchen window above. Nick steps toward her looking up. Back to camera, face to house.

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: But Nick stepped right up to her and said he was the one who did it. She called my Ma, and Ma had to pay for the window.

story_beats.visual_event: BACK to present: Character_P (thin frame, pizza uniform) stands in the apartment kitchen, wiping down the counter, a faint, melancholic smile on his face, lips closed, internal monologue not spoken to another person

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: Somehow Nick gave her cash to pay for it about a week later. I have no idea where a 12-year old came up with sixty bucks, but I was sure proud of him for that. He never told Ma that it was really Mark who did it. None of us did.

story_beats.visual_event: CLOSE-UP on Character_P's hands, still wiping, the memory lingering in his eyes. He sighs softly, a mixture of pride and resignation, lips closed, internal monologue not spoken to another person

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: I guess that's why people respect Nick and our family, even though dad took off. He is a modern-day warrior.

story_beats.visual_event: Character_P turns from the counter, his gaze falling on an old, slightly faded photo on the fridge: Character_N_Young and Character_P_Young, smiling broadly.

music_intent.style_description: Nostalgic, slightly whimsical acoustic folk with a touch of melancholy
music_intent.mood_arc: Viking Roots → Childhood Play → The Incident → Nick's Intervention

scenes.setting: Day 2 - Narrator's Apartment / Metal Factory
scenes.summary: Day 2 - Narrator's Apartment / Metal Factory — Nick has a job down at the local metal factory. He runs one of the assembly lines. Big metal pieces move past and he separates things.

source_excerpts.excerpt:
Total Pages: 94
--- PAGE 1 ---
 Nick and Me

1 
Big brother was mine. I’m not talking about that stupid book we had to read in junior 
high about how the world was gonna turn weird and everybody could see everything you were 
doing all the time. No, I’m talking about my real Big Brother. Nick was the guy everybody was 
scared of. He had a pair on him the size of steel wrecking balls. His face looked like a puzzle 
with the pieces held together with sticky stuff from a glue gun. He wasn’t ugly, just had a few 
brawls over the years to give him that rough look. It was like he rolled out of bed and forgot to 
comb his hair. A few scars here and there, chin and over his left eyebrow. When he walked in the 
bar, or the gym, people took notice, and man, did they move out of the way. I lived with him and 
Ma after dad took off when I was about eight. We lived downtown in a big brick apartment 
building on Kirk Street. A fifth-floor walk-up, so it was a real pain in the ass to haul up the 
groceries and stuff. Luckily, when I went to the store with Nick, he could load up with about six 
of those shopping bags and take them up in one trip, like he was in one of those “world’s 
strongest man” competitions, where they throw boulders over walls, or pull cars with just their 
teeth. I held the rest. 
 One time, when we went into Moxie’s, the gym down on the corner, one of those guys in 
there was doin’ preacher curls with a 40-pound bar on the curl bench. He was right in the middle 
of a set, but as soon 
--- PAGE 2 ---
weight lifting, but I aint gonna curl no fifty pounds as a warm-up. Hell, I aint gonna curl a 50-
pound weight probably ever. That’s okay though, Nick is my brother, like I said. I know he’ll 
stand up for me no matter what. 
 Milwaukee is where we live. It’s where we lived all of our lives. Right there, on Kirk 
Street in that fifth floor apartment. We used to play kick ball in the alley behind the building and 
set up bases with dented-in garbage can lids. One time little Mark Conner kicked a ball right 
through old Mrs. Engel’s kitchen. Man, was she pissed. Mark was scared to death, but Nick 
stepped right up to her and said he was the one who did it. She called my Ma, and Ma had to pay 
for the window. Somehow Nick gave her cash to pay for it about a week later. I have no idea 
where a 12-year old came up with sixty bucks, but I was sure proud of him for that. He never 
told Ma that it was really Mark who did it. None of us did. 
 The thing about Nick was, he was a real-life Viking - you know? What I mean is we had 
these ancestors that came from Norway or Sweden or somewhere over there in Viking land and 
they came over here and stayed in Wisconsin. So, we are Vikings too, I guess. You never hear 
about Vikings, but they are the biggest bad-asses on the planet. I don’t care about gangsters from 
Mexico or China, or even Irish people. Hell, all they do is drink anyways. What I’m talking 
about are tall, strong people who survived in the middle of winter all the time. 
--- PAGE 3 ---
the way those Vikings used to live over there it makes me all proud. They had those huge swords 
and when somebody died, they floated them out to sea on a raft and set them on fire. That’s the 
way it should be. None of this taking up space with caskets and shit. Just set me on fire and float 
me out on a raft. It is so cool to see fire on top of water. 
 I guess that’s why people respect Nick and our family, even though dad took off.

--- PAGE 46 ---
 “No.” she said calmly. “Why do you let other people bother you like that? You know 
they’re just kids, and there’s nothing funny about what we’re doing, so, just ignore them.” 
 “Well I know that logically, but it still pisses me off” I didn’t know how to describe my 
symptoms of inner rage. 
 “Well, I’ll tell you something. Only when we become masters of ourselves, can we really 
deal with the world. What I mean is, the world is full of stupid, violent, seemingly useless 
people. But you aren’t stupid, violent, or useless. You know more than they do. You can see that 
your feelings are not logical, and you can deal with moving on. You have a purpose. We have to 
live with all kinds of people that aren’t as advanced as we are spiritually.” 
 “Do you really think we are more advanced spiritually? How do you know they aren’t 
yet, but might become like Buddha or something?” 
 “So if they do become like Buddha, we should definitely leave them alone, so they can 
follow that path. I mean, if you think one of those boys is going to become the next spiritual 
master, then you should be glad you got a glimpse of what they used to be like!” She just started 
laughing, and so did I. Thinking about one of those numbskulls becoming enlightened made me 
laugh. 
 “You’re really something, you know that?” 
 “You’re really something too.” She looked up at me with her ocean eyes and I leaned 
down and kissed her. That kiss was like slow motion. It was like there was no one else around, 
and
--- PAGE 47 ---
 “That is one of our missions, I think.” She looked pensive. “We have to learn how to 
speak it to everyone, even strangers, and even people we don’t particularly like.” 
 “That’s going to be hard” I said. 
 “I know. That is the meaning of “loving kindness”. That is the ability to open our hearts 
and accept everyone, even those kids on the corner laughing at us. It might take my whole life, 
but I am bound and determined to find that inside of me and bring it out.” 
 “Let me know if you do, because if my girlfriend is loving everyone, I think I’m going to 
need a new one!” I started laughing. 
 “I could never love anyone like I love you.” That was it. That was what had not been 
said, and now it was said. I would remember the way she looked at me for the rest of my life. My 
girlfriend told me she loved me, and it was the most sincere statement I have ever heard. It really 
came from her heart. 
 “I could never love anyone the way I love you either.” We just smiled at each other and 
kept on walking.

--- PAGE 48 ---
 4

Nick was laughing so hard, I thought he might fall off his couch. He had smoke 
sputtering out of the corner of his mouth, and his face was beet red as usual. At that moment I 
wondered why I had even taken the time to come over to his apartment. 
 “Come on, Bro. You haven’t been to my place for ages.” His argument finally persuaded 
me to ride over to his place and sit around with him and a couple old friends before heading to 
Joe’s to play pool and hassle the waitresses. This was the usual scene: Nick invites people over, 
Nick gets hammered, and Nick has an audience to tell his stories to. Boring. I’ve heard all this 
shit about a million times. 
 “Man,” Bob said holding the ceramic pipe and blowing smoke, “I don’t know about you 
guys, but I’m needin’ some new action.

--- PAGE 41 ---
 “So, what’s up with you?” he said, pressing his lips into a strange pursed curl. 
 “Ah, not much. Just working and thinking about going to school” I said. I was expecting 
a huge knee-slapping laugh from Nick, but he just stared at me silently contemplating what I had 
just said. 
 “Going to school? How the hell are you going to pay for that? And what for?” His look 
displayed an incomprehensible expression, as if he had just heard Ma was pregnant or 
something. 
 “Well, I decided that there’s a lot of good jobs out there, but I have to have a degree to do 
anything worth doing, so I’m going to the community college and getting a bachelor’s degree in 
business. Maybe I can manage a restaurant or someplace like that.” I waited for his arguments. 
 “How the hell will you pay for it? How the hell are you going to have time to watch Ma if 
your screwing around with those pencil-pushers?” His eyes looked like they were set on fire. 
 “Sionna told me I could qualify for a low-interest student loan. I checked it all out, and 
applied for it, and they gave me the money. I mean, really, community college isn’t that much 
compared to other places.” 
 Nick cut me short. “Whoa, whoa, whoa dude. You have really done it now. I can’t 
believe you are letting this chick tell you what to do. It’s not like she’s gonna be around to help 
pay this shit back! That’s all on your head man. I can tell you one thing, it sure ain’t gonna be me 
helping your sorry ass out when you finish – IF you fini
--- PAGE 42 ---
want to work in a pizza place my whole life. Second, I don’t do everything Sionna tells me, this 
was my idea. And lastly, it would really mean a lot to me if you could just step up and be on my 
side for once, you know? You are my brother, man.” I was trying not to lose my cool composure, 
because we really needed to work things out. I needed him to work with me on this. 
 “Be on your side? Be on your side?” he said with an elevating voice. 
 “Man, do you actually remember your own life? How many times have I saved your ass? 
I mean really, can you keep count?” 
 Ma was starting to notice there was something unusual going on. She looked up with a 
confused, creased forehead. She started nodding her head side to side, as if to say, “Please don’t 
argue boys.” Slowly, she returned her eyes back to the television screen and folded her hands in 
her lap. 
 “I know, Nick, I know. I’m not saying that. What I mean is, I really need my family to 
support me on this. It’s going to be really hard, and take a lot of time. I will be here for Ma. 
Classes aren’t all day. I have to be in and out of here. I’ll make sure she’s okay.” 
 Nick looked pensive. “Do you remember that time in high school when Donny Amstel 
started harassing you? He was pushing you in the locker room after gym class and calling here 
hanging up, and threatening to steal you bike? Do you remember all that?” 
 “Yeah, sure, I do remember that. You told me you would take care of it, and then, all of a 
sudden, he just 
--- PAGE 43 ---
my body in his front door, so he couldn’t close it. Oh God man, he looked like he was about to 
shit his pants.” Nick started giggling like a little kid. “Then – then, oh man. Then, I told him if he 
ever threatened you like that again he would be killed. I told him our family had mafia 
connections. And he believed me! He was so scared, I couldn’t believe he actually believed me. 
What a dumbass!

scenes.lighting_continuity_token: Early morning light in apartment, harsh industrial lighting in factory
spoiler_constraints: No name tags / personal names on clothing for Character_P (withheld_until_reveal)

story_beats.visual_event: Character_P (28 years old, thin frame, pizza joint uniform), looking tired, helps Character_M (elderly woman, frail, brown hair) sit at the kitchen table, lips closed, internal monologue not spoken to another person

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: Nick has a job down at the local metal factory. He runs one of the assembly lines. Big metal pieces move past and he separates things.

story_beats.visual_event: WIDE SHOT of a bustling, noisy metal factory floor. Character_N (muscular, reddish-brown hair) operates a large crane, moving heavy metal beams with practiced precision, lips closed, internal monologue not spoken to another person

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: Sometimes he runs one of the big cranes inside that lifts stuff up to a higher level. He said it's pretty easy for the money.

story_beats.visual_event: Character_P sits at the kitchen table, head in hands, clearly exhausted. Character_M (elderly, frail, brown hair) stares blankly ahead, lips closed, internal monologue not spoken to another person

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: He lives about five blocks from Ma and me, but he comes over a lot to take care of stuff, so it feels like he still lives here. I wish I made enough money to get my own place.

story_beats.visual_event: CLOSE-UP on Character_M's clouded, distant eyes. Character_P (tired, pizza uniform) gently places a hand on her shoulder, lips closed, internal monologue not spoken to another person

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: Someone has to be here with Ma. She's getting older and it's hard for her to see. Sometimes she gets up in the middle of the night and runs into furniture.

story_beats.visual_event: Character_P (tired, pizza uniform) watches Character_M (elderly, frail, brown hair) slowly shuffle from the living room towards the bedroom, her hand reaching out to steady herself against a wall, lips closed, internal monologue not spoken to another person

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: I don't want her to fall down and nobody knows about it. Ma's had a tough life, tougher than any of us. With dad leaving, then putting up with Nick and me, that's something else.

music_intent.style_description: Driving industrial electronica with a repetitive, almost hypnotic beat, a sense of routine and grind
music_intent.vocal_style: Deep, resonant male voice, spoken word
music_intent.mood_arc: Routine → Nick's Job → Narrator's Burden → Ma's Decline

story_beats.visual_event: Character_P (28 years old, thin frame, pizza uniform) sits at a cluttered desk in his cramped bedroom, staring at a stack of unpaid bills, lips closed, internal monologue not spoken to another person

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: This is the real problem as I see it. I can't afford to go back to school and study to get a 'real' job, because somebody has to take care of Ma.

story_beats.visual_event: Character_N (muscular, reddish-brown hair) stands in the doorway, arms crossed, a cynical smirk on his face, looking down at Character_P (thin frame, pizza uniform).

audio.speaker: Character_N
audio.dialogue: You don't need a degree to get a good job. All you need is to know somebody and you will get a good job someday. That's how it always happens.

story_beats.visual_event: Character_P (thin frame, pizza uniform) looks away, frustrated, then back at Character_N (muscular, reddish-brown hair), who gestures dismissively.

audio.speaker: Character_N
audio.dialogue: What kind of shit do you want to take at school anyway? All that crap is just a bunch of nerds talking to hear themselves talk. Nobody ever learned anything in college that you can use in the real world.

story_beats.visual_event: Character_P (thin frame, pizza uniform) stares at a worn copy of 'Wuthering Heights' on his bedside table, a faint, almost secret smile on his face, lips closed, internal monologue not spoken to another person

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: Nick is right. I am too old. I haven't got the brains to go to school. Besides, I have to take care of Ma and just do the best I can with what I got. I do like reading though. I don't tell nobody that.

music_intent.style_description: Sparse, ambient electronic with a sense of frustration and yearning, slow build
music_intent.mood_arc: The Problem → Nick's View → Narrator's Secret → Outro

scenes.setting: Day 3 - Sidewalk outside Pizza Place
scenes.summary: Day 3 - Sidewalk outside Pizza Place — The reason why I am telling you all this stuff is because something weird is happening to me. I cannot really tell Nick, or Ma, or Bobby down at the pizza joint.

stepped right up to her and said he was the one who did it. She called my Ma, and Ma had to pay 
for the window. Somehow Nick gave her cash to pay for it about a week later. I have no idea 
where a 12-year old came up with sixty bucks, but I was sure proud of him for that. He never 
told Ma that it was really Mark who did it. None of us did. 
 The thing about Nick was, he was a real-life Viking - you know? What I mean is we had 
these ancestors that came from Norway or Sweden or somewhere over there in Viking land and 
they came over here and stayed in Wisconsin. So, we are Vikings too, I guess. You never hear 
about Vikings, but they are the biggest bad-asses on the planet. I don’t care about gangsters from 
Mexico or China, or even Irish people. Hell, all they do is drink anyways. What I’m talking 
about are tall, strong people who survived in the middle of winter all the time. 
--- PAGE 3 ---
the way those Vikings used to live over there it makes me all proud. They had those huge swords 
and when somebody died, they floated them out to sea on a raft and set them on fire. That’s the 
way it should be. None of this taking up space with caskets and shit. Just set me on fire and float 
me out on a raft. It is so cool to see fire on top of water. 
 I guess that’s why people respect Nick and our family, even though dad took off.

--- PAGE 46 ---
 “No.” she said calmly. “Why do you let other people bother you like that? You know 
they’re just kids, and there’s nothing funny about what we’re doing, so, just ignore them.” 
 “Well I know that logically, but it still pisses me off” I didn’t know how to describe my 
symptoms of inner rage. 
 “Well, I’ll tell you something. Only when we become masters of ourselves, can we really 
deal with the world. What I mean is, the world is full of stupid, violent, seemingly useless 
people. But you aren’t stupid, violent, or useless. You know more than they do. You can see that 
your feelings are not logical, and you can deal with moving on. You have a purpose. We have to 
live with all kinds of people that aren’t as advanced as we are spiritually.” 
 “Do you really think we are more advanced spiritually? How do you know they aren’t 
yet, but might become like Buddha or something?” 
 “So if they do become like Buddha, we should definitely leave them alone, so they can 
follow that path. I mean, if you think one of those boys is going to become the next spiritual 
master, then you should be glad you got a glimpse of what they used to be like!” She just started 
laughing, and so did I. Thinking about one of those numbskulls becoming enlightened made me 
laugh. 
 “You’re really something, you know that?” 
 “You’re really something too.” She looked up at me with her ocean eyes and I leaned 
down and kissed her. That kiss was like slow motion. It was like there was no one else around, 
and
--- PAGE 47 ---
 “That is one of our missions, I think.” She looked pensive. “We have to learn how to 
speak it to everyone, even strangers, and even people we don’t particularly like.” 
 “That’s going to be hard” I said. 
 “I know. That is the meaning of “loving kindness”. That is the ability to open our hearts 
and accept everyone, even those kids on the corner laughing at us. It might take my whole life, 
but I am bound and determined to find that inside of me and bring it out.” 
 “Let me know if you do, because if my girlfriend is loving everyone, I think I’m going to 
need a new one!” I started laughing. 
 “I could never love anyone like I love you.” That was it. That was what had not been 
said, and now it was said. I would remember the way she looked at me for the rest of my life. My 
girlfriend told me she loved me, and it was the most sincere statement I have ever heard. It really 
came from her heart. 
 “I could never love anyone the way I love you either.” We just smiled at each other and 
kept on walking.

--- PAGE 48 ---
 4

Nick was laughing so hard, I thought he might fall off his couch. He had smoke 
sputtering out of the corner of his mouth, and his face was beet red as usual. At that moment I 
wondered why I had even taken the time to come over to his apartment. 
 “Come on, Bro. You haven’t been to my place for ages.” His argument finally persuaded 
me to ride over to his place and sit around with him and a couple old friends before heading to 
Joe’s to play pool and hassle the waitresses. This was the usual scene: Nick invites people over, 
Nick gets hammered, and Nick has an audience to tell his stories to. Boring. I’ve heard all this 
shit about a million times. 
 “Man,” Bob said holding the ceramic pipe and blowing smoke, “I don’t know about you 
guys, but I’m needin’ some new action.

--- PAGE 41 ---
 “So, what’s up with you?” he said, pressing his lips into a strange pursed curl. 
 “Ah, not much. Just working and thinking about going to school” I said. I was expecting 
a huge knee-slapping laugh from Nick, but he just stared at me silently contemplating what I had 
just said. 
 “Going to school? How the hell are you going to pay for that? And what for?” His look 
displayed an incomprehensible expression, as if he had just heard Ma was pregnant or 
something. 
 “Well, I decided that there’s a lot of good jobs out there, but I have to have a degree to do 
anything worth doing, so I’m going to the community college and getting a bachelor’s degree in 
business. Maybe I can manage a restaurant or someplace like that.” I waited for his arguments. 
 “How the hell will you pay for it? How the hell are you going to have time to watch Ma if 
your screwing around with those pencil-pushers?” His eyes looked like they were set on fire. 
 “Sionna told me I could qualify for a low-interest student loan. I checked it all out, and 
applied for it, and they gave me the money. I mean, really, community college isn’t that much 
compared to other places.” 
 Nick cut me short. “Whoa, whoa, whoa dude. You have really done it now. I can’t 
believe you are letting this chick tell you what to do. It’s not like she’s gonna be around to help 
pay this shit back! That’s all on your head man. I can tell you one thing, it sure ain’t gonna be me 
helping your sorry ass out when you finish – IF you fini
--- PAGE 42 ---
want to work in a pizza place my whole life. Second, I don’t do everything Sionna tells me, this 
was my idea. And lastly, it would really mean a lot to me if you could just step up and be on my 
side for once, you know? You are my brother, man.” I was trying not to lose my cool composure, 
because we really needed to work things out. I needed him to work with me on this. 
 “Be on your side? Be on your side?” he said with an elevating voice. 
 “Man, do you actually remember your own life? How many times have I saved your ass? 
I mean really, can you keep count?” 
 Ma was starting to notice there was something unusual going on. She looked up with a 
confused, creased forehead. She started nodding her head side to side, as if to say, “Please don’t 
argue boys.” Slowly, she returned her eyes back to the television screen and folded her hands in 
her lap. 
 “I know, Nick, I know. I’m not saying that. What I mean is, I really need my family to 
support me on this. It’s going to be really hard, and take a lot of time. I will be here for Ma. 
Classes aren’t all day. I have to be in and out of here. I’ll make sure she’s okay.” 
 Nick looked pensive. “Do you remember that time in high school when Donny Amstel 
started harassing you? He was pushing you in the locker room after gym class and calling here 
hanging up, and threatening to steal you bike? Do you remember all that?” 
 “Yeah, sure, I do remember that. You told me you would take care of it, and then, all of a 
sudden, he just 
--- PAGE 43 ---
my body in his front door, so he couldn’t close it. Oh God man, he looked like he was about to 
shit his pants.” Nick started giggling like a little kid. “Then – then, oh man. Then, I told him if he 
ever threatened you like that again he would be killed. I told him our family had mafia 
connections. And he believed me! He was so scared, I couldn’t believe he actually believed me. 
What a dumbass!

source_excerpts.excerpt:
--- PAGE 6 ---
2

The reason why I’m telling you all this stuff, is because something weird is happening to 
me. I can’t really tell Nick, or Ma, or Bobby down at the pizza joint, because they wouldn’t 
understand. Hell, I don’t understand what’s happening. 
See, this one day I rode my bike to work, and just as I was walking down the sidewalk 
and into the front door of the pizza place, this girl tripped over the cement while she was walking 
toward me. She kind of fell into me. She fell and just kind of brushed into me with her shoulder. 
She had kind of short brown hair and was skinny. Not too skinny, but good looking in a way. 
The first thing I did was say, “Are you okay?” and she said, “Yes” and looked down at herself 
and brushed off her shirt. She was wearing a black short sleeved shirt with a V-neck. The kind of 
shirt you can get real cheap at Target or Walmart. She looked pretty good. She had on a necklace 
with some kind of fish symbol on it. She had jeans, and blue tennis shoes and a big backpack. 
We just kind of looked at each other and didn’t know what to say. So I finally said, “I 
work here, I’m just going to work. What is your name?” 
“Sionna” she said. 
“Sionna? That’s different. I never heard of that. What does that mean?” 
She started to tell me that her mom and dad named her after an Irish goddess, and then 
something about the River Shannon. I wasn’t really listening though, because she had the coolest 
eyes I ever saw. They were a real light blue color, and rem
--- PAGE 7 ---
Not that she was really asking me out, but it kind of felt like that to me. It came out of her like it 
was no big deal. She spoke like cool blue, like the cool blue of her eyes. I got her phone number 
and told her I would call her later on. 
Now, this might not seem weird to you, but like I said no girl has ever been interested in 
me. She was not what I would describe as being “cute” but I would say she was “pretty”. She 
had that real pale skin, the kind you would see in some kind of English story. Like one of those 
old stories from a long time ago, when women wore big huge dresses and piled their hair up on 
their heads. She should have been holding a fan or something. 
When she walked away from me, she looked back over her shoulder kind of, like she was 
trying to remember what I was going to look like tomorrow when she wasn’t seeing me. It made 
me feel a little weird inside, like someone was lookin’ right through me or something. That 
wasn’t the weirdest thing though- here’s the weird thing that I can’t tell nobody. It was like I 
“knew” her or something. Like I had seen her somewhere before. But I didn’t, I would have 
remembered somebody like her. She seemed mysterious. I guess I would use a word here I have 
heard – intense-yea, that’s it, she was intense. 
I just watched her walk away. She moved real smooth. It was like there wasn’t any bones 
in her body. It reminded me of the waves I saw when I went to the ocean out by New Jersey 
once. We had an aunt and uncl
--- PAGE 8 ---
That was like Sionna, except she was washing up on me, and I didn’t want to move my feet, I 
just wanted her to cover me completely.

3 
 That day I met Sionna, when I got home, Ma was in a crazy mood. She was looking 
around the house mumbling to herself about trying to find her pain pills. She had her pills all out 
on the counter in a straight line so we didn’t have to mess with trying to figure out what to give 
her everyday.

bar, or the gym, people took notice, and man, did they move out of the way. I lived with him and 
Ma after dad took off when I was about eight. We lived downtown in a big brick apartment 
building on Kirk Street. A fifth-floor walk-up, so it was a real pain in the ass to haul up the 
groceries and stuff. Luckily, when I went to the store with Nick, he could load up with about six 
of those shopping bags and take them up in one trip, like he was in one of those “world’s 
strongest man” competitions, where they throw boulders over walls, or pull cars with just their 
teeth. I held the rest.

her lap. 
 “I know, Nick, I know. I’m not saying that. What I mean is, I really need my family to 
support me on this. It’s going to be really hard, and take a lot of time. I will be here for Ma. 
Classes aren’t all day. I have to be in and out of here. I’ll make sure she’s okay.” 
 Nick looked pensive. “Do you remember that time in high school when Donny Amstel 
started harassing you? He was pushing you in the locker room after gym class and calling here 
hanging up, and threatening to steal you bike? Do you remember all that?” 
 “Yeah, sure, I do remember that. You told me you would take care of it, and then, all of a 
sudden, he just 
--- PAGE 43 ---
my body in his front door, so he couldn’t close it. Oh God man, he looked like he was about to 
shit his pants.” Nick started giggling like a little kid. “Then – then, oh man. Then, I told him if he 
ever threatened you like that again he would be killed. I told him our family had mafia 
connections. And he believed me! He was so scared, I couldn’t believe he actually believed me. 
What a dumbass!

1.

It was the most perfect spring day. We were at the tail-end of the school year with only a week 
before summer break. The lilac bushes were blooming at the back edge of the school property. I 
was almost out of fourth grade. Me and Bobby Swenson were laughing our asses off, folded over 
at the waist, grabbing our sides before they split out. Jennifer Bales and Sue Something-or-other 
were looking at us with stern faces and shaking their heads. 
 “You guys are in so much trouble when Mrs. Stark finds out you’re making fun of her” 
Jennifer said with her know-it-all voice. She was like a forty year old stuck in the body of a nine 
year old. Her brown braids ended halfway down her back with the flail of red bows that bounced 
off her when she walked. She and Sue walked toward the building, probably on the way to report 
our goofy behavior to Mrs. Stark. 
 “You HAVE to learn some responsibility!” I cried, barely able to spit the words out 
through the raucous laughter. 
 “No, YOU have to learn some responsibility!” Bobby spit out, his face red and dribble 
running out of his mouth from the uncontrolled giggling. 
 “Learn some responsibility, learn some responsibility!” we cried in unison, mocking Mrs. 
Stark’s earlier comment in the classroom directed towards us both. We had been sending notes 
under the desk to one another, putting pencil eraser tips up our noses, and acting like 
tyrannosaurus rex monsters coming to life. Generally the type of t
--- PAGE 36 ---
school year when we got tired of long division and trying to write paragraphs with three parts. It 
was total relief. Total uncontrollable joy bubbling up from the far reaches inside, telling the 
world “we’re almost free! Hee hee!” 
 Apparently Mrs. Stark didn’t see it that way. We had been getting on her bad side for the 
last week or so. She was at the end of her rope. Ma got a call from her the other night, and told 
me if I didn’t straighten out and “fly right” I would be punished. She would take my bike away 
for a week. Crap, I didn’t want that to happen. But, I just couldn’t help myself today. Everything 
Bobby and I saw, or heard, was absolutely hilarious. I saw Nick from the corner of my eye. He 
was walking toward us; hair in a devilish mess. “What are you two numbskulls doin’?” he asked 
looking around for a clue. 
 Bobby just looked at me nervously, seemingly muted by Nick’s presence. Nick was a big 
bad-assed sixth-grader. He was at the top of the totem pole in our school. Not only was he in the 
senior class, he was tall, and big, and highly respected for his bravery in every situation. Since 
Bobby wasn’t going to answer, I said, “Oh, just the usual crap from Mrs. Stark. She thinks we 
need to learn some responsibility”. The last two words came out slow with a muffled sputter 
before I burst into laughter again, my face red, and tears starting to form in the corners of my 
eyes. Nick slapped me across the shoulder. “Little Bro, you better start listening, man.
--- PAGE 37 ---
Mr. Pauls was the principal, and he sure wasn’t a “pal”. He was kind of a big prick. He didn’t 
like getting into it with arguing kids, and he really didn’t like getting into it with disrespectful 
kids and their parents. 
 “Um, sorry Mrs. Stark” Nick interrupted. “He didn’t mean anything by it. He and Bobby 
were just goofing around. You should see them around my house. It never ends. I’ll talk to him 
and Ma will talk to him. He’ll never act up in class again. I guarantee it.” 
 Mrs.

scenes.setting: Day 3 - Joe's Bar
scenes.summary: Day 3 - Joe's Bar — What's the matter? I can tell you're hidin' something. What's goin' on?

source_excerpts.excerpt:
--- PAGE 6 ---
2

The reason why I’m telling you all this stuff, is because something weird is happening to 
me. I can’t really tell Nick, or Ma, or Bobby down at the pizza joint, because they wouldn’t 
understand. Hell, I don’t understand what’s happening. 
See, this one day I rode my bike to work, and just as I was walking down the sidewalk 
and into the front door of the pizza place, this girl tripped over the cement while she was walking 
toward me. She kind of fell into me. She fell and just kind of brushed into me with her shoulder. 
She had kind of short brown hair and was skinny. Not too skinny, but good looking in a way. 
The first thing I did was say, “Are you okay?” and she said, “Yes” and looked down at herself 
and brushed off her shirt. She was wearing a black short sleeved shirt with a V-neck. The kind of 
shirt you can get real cheap at Target or Walmart. She looked pretty good. She had on a necklace 
with some kind of fish symbol on it. She had jeans, and blue tennis shoes and a big backpack. 
We just kind of looked at each other and didn’t know what to say. So I finally said, “I 
work here, I’m just going to work. What is your name?” 
“Sionna” she said. 
“Sionna? That’s different. I never heard of that. What does that mean?” 
She started to tell me that her mom and dad named her after an Irish goddess, and then 
something about the River Shannon. I wasn’t really listening though, because she had the coolest 
eyes I ever saw. They were a real light blue color, and rem
--- PAGE 7 ---
Not that she was really asking me out, but it kind of felt like that to me. It came out of her like it 
was no big deal. She spoke like cool blue, like the cool blue of her eyes. I got her phone number 
and told her I would call her later on. 
Now, this might not seem weird to you, but like I said no girl has ever been interested in 
me. She was not what I would describe as being “cute” but I would say she was “pretty”. She 
had that real pale skin, the kind you would see in some kind of English story. Like one of those 
old stories from a long time ago, when women wore big huge dresses and piled their hair up on 
their heads. She should have been holding a fan or something. 
When she walked away from me, she looked back over her shoulder kind of, like she was 
trying to remember what I was going to look like tomorrow when she wasn’t seeing me. It made 
me feel a little weird inside, like someone was lookin’ right through me or something. That 
wasn’t the weirdest thing though- here’s the weird thing that I can’t tell nobody. It was like I 
“knew” her or something. Like I had seen her somewhere before. But I didn’t, I would have 
remembered somebody like her. She seemed mysterious. I guess I would use a word here I have 
heard – intense-yea, that’s it, she was intense. 
I just watched her walk away. She moved real smooth. It was like there wasn’t any bones 
in her body. It reminded me of the waves I saw when I went to the ocean out by New Jersey 
once. We had an aunt and uncl
--- PAGE 8 ---
That was like Sionna, except she was washing up on me, and I didn’t want to move my feet, I 
just wanted her to cover me completely.

3 
 That day I met Sionna, when I got home, Ma was in a crazy mood. She was looking 
around the house mumbling to herself about trying to find her pain pills. She had her pills all out 
on the counter in a straight line so we didn’t have to mess with trying to figure out what to give 
her everyday.

--- PAGE 46 ---
 “No.” she said calmly. “Why do you let other people bother you like that? You know 
they’re just kids, and there’s nothing funny about what we’re doing, so, just ignore them.” 
 “Well I know that logically, but it still pisses me off” I didn’t know how to describe my 
symptoms of inner rage. 
 “Well, I’ll tell you something. Only when we become masters of ourselves, can we really 
deal with the world. What I mean is, the world is full of stupid, violent, seemingly useless 
people. But you aren’t stupid, violent, or useless. You know more than they do. You can see that 
your feelings are not logical, and you can deal with moving on. You have a purpose. We have to 
live with all kinds of people that aren’t as advanced as we are spiritually.” 
 “Do you really think we are more advanced spiritually? How do you know they aren’t 
yet, but might become like Buddha or something?” 
 “So if they do become like Buddha, we should definitely leave them alone, so they can 
follow that path. I mean, if you think one of those boys is going to become the next spiritual 
master, then you should be glad you got a glimpse of what they used to be like!” She just started 
laughing, and so did I. Thinking about one of those numbskulls becoming enlightened made me 
laugh. 
 “You’re really something, you know that?” 
 “You’re really something too.” She looked up at me with her ocean eyes and I leaned 
down and kissed her. That kiss was like slow motion. It was like there was no one else around, 
and
--- PAGE 47 ---
 “That is one of our missions, I think.” She looked pensive. “We have to learn how to 
speak it to everyone, even strangers, and even people we don’t particularly like.” 
 “That’s going to be hard” I said. 
 “I know. That is the meaning of “loving kindness”. That is the ability to open our hearts 
and accept everyone, even those kids on the corner laughing at us. It might take my whole life, 
but I am bound and determined to find that inside of me and bring it out.” 
 “Let me know if you do, because if my girlfriend is loving everyone, I think I’m going to 
need a new one!” I started laughing. 
 “I could never love anyone like I love you.” That was it. That was what had not been 
said, and now it was said. I would remember the way she looked at me for the rest of my life. My 
girlfriend told me she loved me, and it was the most sincere statement I have ever heard. It really 
came from her heart. 
 “I could never love anyone the way I love you either.” We just smiled at each other and 
kept on walking.

--- PAGE 48 ---
 4

Nick was laughing so hard, I thought he might fall off his couch. He had smoke 
sputtering out of the corner of his mouth, and his face was beet red as usual. At that moment I 
wondered why I had even taken the time to come over to his apartment. 
 “Come on, Bro. You haven’t been to my place for ages.” His argument finally persuaded 
me to ride over to his place and sit around with him and a couple old friends before heading to 
Joe’s to play pool and hassle the waitresses. This was the usual scene: Nick invites people over, 
Nick gets hammered, and Nick has an audience to tell his stories to. Boring. I’ve heard all this 
shit about a million times. 
 “Man,” Bob said holding the ceramic pipe and blowing smoke, “I don’t know about you 
guys, but I’m needin’ some new action.

--- PAGE 41 ---
 “So, what’s up with you?” he said, pressing his lips into a strange pursed curl. 
 “Ah, not much. Just working and thinking about going to school” I said. I was expecting 
a huge knee-slapping laugh from Nick, but he just stared at me silently contemplating what I had 
just said. 
 “Going to school? How the hell are you going to pay for that? And what for?” His look 
displayed an incomprehensible expression, as if he had just heard Ma was pregnant or 
something. 
 “Well, I decided that there’s a lot of good jobs out there, but I have to have a degree to do 
anything worth doing, so I’m going to the community college and getting a bachelor’s degree in 
business. Maybe I can manage a restaurant or someplace like that.” I waited for his arguments. 
 “How the hell will you pay for it? How the hell are you going to have time to watch Ma if 
your screwing around with those pencil-pushers?” His eyes looked like they were set on fire. 
 “Sionna told me I could qualify for a low-interest student loan. I checked it all out, and 
applied for it, and they gave me the money. I mean, really, community college isn’t that much 
compared to other places.” 
 Nick cut me short. “Whoa, whoa, whoa dude. You have really done it now. I can’t 
believe you are letting this chick tell you what to do. It’s not like she’s gonna be around to help 
pay this shit back! That’s all on your head man. I can tell you one thing, it sure ain’t gonna be me 
helping your sorry ass out when you finish – IF you fini
--- PAGE 42 ---
want to work in a pizza place my whole life. Second, I don’t do everything Sionna tells me, this 
was my idea. And lastly, it would really mean a lot to me if you could just step up and be on my 
side for once, you know? You are my brother, man.” I was trying not to lose my cool composure, 
because we really needed to work things out. I needed him to work with me on this. 
 “Be on your side? Be on your side?” he said with an elevating voice. 
 “Man, do you actually remember your own life? How many times have I saved your ass? 
I mean really, can you keep count?” 
 Ma was starting to notice there was something unusual going on. She looked up with a 
confused, creased forehead. She started nodding her head side to side, as if to say, “Please don’t 
argue boys.” Slowly, she returned her eyes back to the television screen and folded her hands in 
her lap. 
 “I know, Nick, I know. I’m not saying that. What I mean is, I really need my family to 
support me on this. It’s going to be really hard, and take a lot of time. I will be here for Ma. 
Classes aren’t all day. I have to be in and out of here. I’ll make sure she’s okay.” 
 Nick looked pensive. “Do you remember that time in high school when Donny Amstel 
started harassing you? He was pushing you in the locker room after gym class and calling here 
hanging up, and threatening to steal you bike? Do you remember all that?” 
 “Yeah, sure, I do remember that. You told me you would take care of it, and then, all of a 
sudden, he just 
--- PAGE 43 ---
my body in his front door, so he couldn’t close it. Oh God man, he looked like he was about to 
shit his pants.” Nick started giggling like a little kid. “Then – then, oh man. Then, I told him if he 
ever threatened you like that again he would be killed. I told him our family had mafia 
connections. And he believed me! He was so scared, I couldn’t believe he actually believed me. 
What a dumbass!

story_beats.visual_event: Character_P (thin frame, pizza uniform) recounts the story of Character_M's confusion, looking distressed and trying to appease Character_N (muscular, reddish-brown hair), who is now clenching his fists, lips closed, internal monologue not spoken to another person

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: I told him the story of Ma being all confused. Like I thought, he started getting pissed off.

story_beats.visual_event: Character_N (muscular, reddish-brown hair) explodes, slamming his hand on the bar. Character_P (thin frame, pizza uniform) flinches. Background patrons at Joe's Bar glance over nervously; show clear progressive motion of the main action, not a frozen pose

audio.speaker: Character_N
audio.dialogue: I knew she would start going crazy cooped up in that place all day. Why don't you take her to the store or something? Holy Christ, do something with her before she turns into some demented old fart!

story_beats.visual_event: Character_P (thin frame, pizza uniform) looks utterly defeated, staring into his beer glass. His internal turmoil is evident on his face, lips closed, internal monologue not spoken to another person

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: Of course not. I just needed to get away from her for a while, that's all. God, sometimes I really hate Nick inside. I mean, he's done so much for us, but at the same time, inside I really get tired of his shit.

story_beats.visual_event: Character_P (thin frame, pizza uniform) stands up from the bar, leaving his beer, and walks quickly towards the exit, his gaze fixed on the library sign across the street, lips closed, internal monologue not spoken to another person

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: It was freakin' cold outside, and I walked a little faster than normal. When I looked up, I saw the library across the street and just halfway down the block. I should go in there, I thought. Who's gonna know anyway?

scenes.setting: Day 3 - Public Library (Interior)
scenes.summary: Day 3 - Public Library (Interior) — I walked real fast over there and saw they were open till 9 o’clock, so I had a little time. The place was warm and quiet, like a church but without the guilt.

“Do you have a library card?” she asked. 
“No, how do I get one of those?” 
She showed me this piece of paper and I wrote down my name and address. Then she 
gave me a “temporary” paper card. She said a plastic one would be mailed to me within the 
week. That was cool, cause I got the mail anyway. It was like I could get anything in here for 
free. Who needed school anyway when you could get the same thing for free in the library? What 
stupid shit spends his money on classes when everything is right here, in this building?

I had no idea what I wanted in this place, but I just started walking down the rows

I just picked out “Wuthering Heights” by a lady named Emily Bronte. I checked out the book 
with the lady at the desk, who gave me a thin little smile, and then walked home in the cold. 
Shit, I missed the whole game. Just as well, Chicago won by a touchdown anyway. Ma 
was already in bed when I got there. Since there was nothing to do, I started reading that book. It

audio.dialogue: I walked real fast over there and saw they were open till 9 o’clock, so I had a little time. The place was warm and quiet, like a church but without the guilt.

story_beats.visual_event: Character_P (28 years old, thin frame, blue pizza uniform shirt) stands at the wooden reception desk. A middle-aged female librarian (brown hair in a bun, glasses, knit cardigan) hands him a pen and a paper registration form, lips closed, internal monologue not spoken to another person

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: I asked the lady at the desk how to check out a book, and she asked if I had a card. No, how do I get one of those?

story_beats.visual_event: Character_P (28 years old, thin frame, blue pizza uniform shirt) fills out the form at the desk. The librarian (knit cardigan, glasses) smiles gently and hands him a temporary paper library card, lips closed, internal monologue not spoken to another person

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: She showed me this piece of paper and I wrote down my name and address. Then she gave me a temporary paper card. She said a plastic one would be mailed to me within the week. That was cool, cause I got the mail anyway.

story_beats.visual_event: Character_P (28 years old, thin frame, blue pizza uniform shirt) wanders slowly down a dimly lit library aisle labeled 'Classic Literature'. His fingers trace the spines of old books on the shelves, lips closed, internal monologue not spoken to another person

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: I had no idea what I wanted in this place, but I just started walking down the rows looking at all the books. For some reason I ended up in the classic literature area.

story_beats.visual_event: Character_P (28 years old, thin frame, blue pizza uniform shirt) pulls out a worn paperback copy of 'Wuthering Heights' from the shelf. He holds it in his hand, looking down at the cover with curiosity, lips closed, internal monologue not spoken to another person

audio.speaker: Character_P
audio.dialogue: There was so many I didn't know which one to pick, but I thought they were all important if they were in that section, so I just picked out Wuthering Heights by a lady named Emily Bronte. I checked out the book and walked home in the cold.

music_intent.style_description: Quiet, atmospheric acoustic guitar and ambient keyboard pads, creating a peaceful, contemplative mood
music_intent.mood_arc: Library Entry → The Card → Wandering Rows → Discovery

scenes.setting: Day 4 - Narrator's Apartment (Kitchen)
scenes.summary: Day 4 - Narrator's Apartment (Kitchen) — The next day I woke up and Ma was at the kitchen table drinking her coffee. We sat there as usual just looking at each other with nothing to say really.

source_excerpts.excerpt:
--- PAGE 11 ---
“Do you have a library card?” she asked. 
“No, how do I get one of those?” 
She showed me this piece of paper and I wrote down my name and address. Then she 
gave me a “temporary” paper card. She said a plastic one would be mailed to me within the 
week. That was cool, cause I got the mail anyway. It was like I could get anything in here for 
free. Who needed school anyway when you could get the same thing for free in the library? What 
stupid shit spends his money on classes when everything is right here, in this building?

I had no idea what I wanted in this place, but I just started walking down the rows 
looking at all the books. For some reason I ended up in the “classic literature” area and saw all 
these books by Shakespeare, Dickens, Eliot, Dumas, and Wilde. There was so many I didn’t 
know which one to pick out, but I thought they were all important if they were in that section, so 
I just picked out “Wuthering Heights” by a lady named Emily Bronte. I checked out the book 
with the lady at the desk, who gave me a thin little smile, and then walked home in the cold. 
Shit, I missed the whole game. Just as well, Chicago won by a touchdown anyway. Ma 
was already in bed when I got there. Since there was nothing to do, I started reading that book. It 
was pretty weird at first, because it was about a place in England a long time ago. It was about a 
guy named Heathcliff and a girl named Catherine. So far, it was a little like a soap opera. There 
was a lot of people 
--- PAGE 12 ---
wearing old clothes, and riding horses and running through the outdoors all happy. I can’t 
remember if I was in this or not. I just got this feeling though that I would end up feeling for her 
just like Heathcliff did in the book.

4

The next day I woke up and Ma was at the kitchen table drinking her coffee. We sat there 
as usual just looking at each other with nothing to say really. I asked her what she did last night. 
Why didn’t she stay up till I got home? I tried to talk her into going for a walk. You would have 
thought I asked her to run a marathon or something. She gave me every excuse in the book. 
 “It’s so cold outside. I don’t have a nice coat to wear. Where are we going anyway?” It 
just went on and on and on. Finally, I just gave up. If Nick wants her to go outside so bad, then 
he can drag his ass over here and get her to go. 
 Of course, she probably would if Nick asked her. She listens to everything he says. He 
could ask her to swim across Lake Michigan, and she would probably try it for him. I swear 
sometimes, there’s no reason for me being born anyway, except to have somebody for Nick to 
boss around. One time, Nick told her he was getting serious about this girl he had. Like that was 
really going to happen. Anyways, Ma went so out of her way to smother this girl, it was so 
ridiculous. Having her over for steak dinners, inviting her for Thanksgiving, and trying to meet 
her parents – the whole deal. She was so deep in this shit that whe
--- PAGE 13 ---
she went haywire. She didn’t stop crying and sobbing for a week. I think she thinks she’s never 
having any grandkids. 
 The more I thought about that whole situation, the more pissed off I got, so I told Ma I 
was leaving for a little while. I would see her in a couple hours. When I got outside, I found the 
phone number Sionna gave me. I was shaking and wondering if she would even remember me at 
all. 
 “Hi.” 
“Hi, Sionna?

3 
 That day I met Sionna, when I got home, Ma was in a crazy mood. She was looking 
around the house mumbling to herself about trying to find her pain pills. She had her pills all out 
on the counter in a straight line so we didn’t have to mess with trying to figure out what to give 
her everyday. She would take a pill from the front bottle, move it to the back of the line, and so 
on. She did pretty good with it. That was one thing she did remember. That way, I didn’t have to 
be around constantly telling her what she was supposed to be taking. 
 She knew about this, but seemed confused. 
“Ma, what’s the matter?” I asked. 
“I can’t find my pain pills, I know I put them here somewhere. Did we go to church 
today? Did you see Mrs. Canasta there?” 
Okay, this was definitely weird. We hadn’t been to church for over fifteen years, and I 
aint seen Mrs. Canasta for at least the last year. 
--- PAGE 9 ---
“Ma, what are you talking about? We don’t go to church anymore. Mrs. Canasta lives 
down one level, all’s you have to do is walk down there and see her if you want. Your pills are 
right in front of you in the box for today.” 
She gave me this look I never saw before from Ma. Her eyes were all glassy and looked 
really empty. It was like she didn’t know who I was or something. She snapped out of it though, 
and said, “Oh, right, I am so sorry. I see them right here. I don’t know what got into me.” 
Wow, she was losing it. I would have to tell Nick about this. I’m sure he’ll get mad or 
something –like it was my fault. Later on I went down to Joe’s to get a beer. I had to get out of 
the apartment. Ma was freaking me out. Chances are, I would see Nick down there. He always 
came down on Mondays to watch football on T.V. Everybody would sit around, or play pool, or 
darts, and start B.Sing about crap that went on that day. It made Mondays not seem so bad after 
all. 
So I was sitting at the bar watching the Packers game – this was a big one against the 
Bears –when Nick came in. He slapped me on the back. 
“Hey, little brother. What’s shakin’?” he said. 
“I don’t know. Nothin’.” 
“What’s the matter? I can tell you’re hidin’ something. What’s goin’ on?” 
I told him the story of Ma being all confused. Like I thought, he started getting pissed off. 
“I knew she would start going crazy cooped up in that place all day” he said. “Why don’t you 
take her to the store or something? H
--- PAGE 10 ---
“Of course not. I just needed to get away from her for a while, that’s all.” He gave me 
that look like he wanted to punch me in the face. It was a little scary. I don’t think he would 
actually do it, but I’ve seen what he can do, and I don’t want that damage. He was right, of 
course, I did need to get home and watch Ma. I paid for my beer and told him I would watch it at 
home with her and I promised to get her out tomorrow and at least take a walk. 
“That’s more like it,” he said. 
God, sometimes I really hate Nick inside. I mean, he’s done so much for us, but at the 
same time, inside I really get tired of his shit. I would never tell anybody this, but I do get tired 
of it. It was freakin’ cold outside, and I walked a little faster than normal. When I looked up, I 
saw the library across the street and just halfway down the block. I should go in there, I thought. 
Who’s gonna know anyway? Ma can barely remember what the hell is going on, and Nick won’t 
know.

--- PAGE 46 ---
 “No.” she said calmly. “Why do you let other people bother you like that? You know 
they’re just kids, and there’s nothing funny about what we’re doing, so, just ignore them.” 
 “Well I know that logically, but it still pisses me off” I didn’t know how to describe my 
symptoms of inner rage. 
 “Well, I’ll tell you something. Only when we become masters of ourselves, can we really 
deal with the world. What I mean is, the world is full of stupid, violent, seemingly useless 
people. But you aren’t stupid, violent, or useless. You know more than they do. You can see that 
your feelings are not logical, and you can deal with moving on. You have a purpose. We have to 
live with all kinds of people that aren’t as advanced as we are spiritually.” 
 “Do you really think we are more advanced spiritually? How do you know they aren’t 
yet, but might become like Buddha or something?” 
 “So if they do become like Buddha, we should definitely leave them alone, so they can 
follow that path. I mean, if you think one of those boys is going to become the next spiritual 
master, then you should be glad you got a glimpse of what they used to be like!” She just started 
laughing, and so did I. Thinking about one of those numbskulls becoming enlightened made me 
laugh. 
 “You’re really something, you know that?” 
 “You’re really something too.” She looked up at me with her ocean eyes and I leaned 
down and kissed her. That kiss was like slow motion. It was like there was no one else around, 
and
--- PAGE 47 ---
 “That is one of our missions, I think.” She looked pensive. “We have to learn how to 
speak it to everyone, even strangers, and even people we don’t particularly like.” 
 “That’s going to be hard” I said. 
 “I know. That is the meaning of “loving kindness”. That is the ability to open our hearts 
and accept everyone, even those kids on the corner laughing at us. It might take my whole life, 
but I am bound and determined to find that inside of me and bring it out.” 
 “Let me know if you do, because if my girlfriend is loving everyone, I think I’m going to 
need a new one!” I started laughing. 
 “I could never love anyone like I love you.” That was it. That was what had not been 
said, and now it was said. I would remember the way she looked at me for the rest of my life. My 
girlfriend told me she loved me, and it was the most sincere statement I have ever heard. It really 
came from her heart. 
 “I could never love anyone the way I love you either.” We just smiled at each other and 
kept on walking.

--- PAGE 48 ---
 4

Nick was laughing so hard, I thought he might fall off his couch. He had smoke 
sputtering out of the corner of his mouth, and his face was beet red as usual. At that moment I 
wondered why I had even taken the time to come over to his apartment. 
 “Come on, Bro. You haven’t been to my place for ages.” His argument finally persuaded 
me to ride over to his place and sit around with him and a couple old friends before heading to 
Joe’s to play pool and hassle the waitresses. This was the usual scene: Nick invites people over, 
Nick gets hammered, and Nick has an audience to tell his stories to. Boring. I’ve heard all this 
shit about a million times. 
 “Man,” Bob said holding the ceramic pipe and blowing smoke, “I don’t know about you 
guys, but I’m needin’ some new action.
